backgroundImageAltText: Medical education diagram with interactive hotspots

params.text:
Perforating veins contain bicuspid valves permitting flow only from superficial to deep.

Clinical Pearl: When valves fail, hydrostatic pressure transmits to superficial veins, causing varicosities.

Memory Aid: "S→D = Safe Direction" — Superficial to Deep is normal flow.

Cockett's perforators (lower 1/3 leg) and Boyd's perforator (below knee) connect the great saphenous vein to posterior tibial veins.

Clinical Pearl: Cockett's perforators at 7, 13, and 18 cm above the medial malleolus — a classic exam question.

Memory Aid: "7-13-18 — Cockett's gate."

When perforator valves become incompetent, retrograde flow occurs — blood flows backwards from deep to superficial.

Clinical Pearl: Chronic venous hypertension causes pigmentation, lipodermatosclerosis, and venous ulceration near the medial malleolus.

Memory Aid: "Incompetent valve = Inverted flow."

Venous ulcers are typically shallow, irregular, and above the medial malleolus.

Clinical Pearl: Key differential: venous ulcers are medial, arterial ulcers are on toes/pressure points.

Memory Aid: "Medial Malleolus = Venous territory."

Differentiates SFJ incompetence from perforator incompetence. Tourniquet at SFJ, patient stands — if veins fill from below, perforators are incompetent.

Clinical Pearl: Positive test (rapid filling despite tourniquet) = perforator incompetence.

Memory Aid: "Tourniquet Traps the Top" — if blood still arrives from below, the problem is the perforators.
